description: Portability scope of the unit relative to user, project, machine, and session contexts. The 5-value enum from R-frpg, confirmed as load-bearing across all 13 Wave 1 agents.
rationale: Confirmed by all 13 Wave 1 agents. R-frpg demonstrates 5-system convergence on the same enum (chezmoi, VSCode, Nx, XDG, Agent Skills). Load-bearing for every sync decision: only universal + user files travel; project + machine + ephemeral stay local. D2 surfaced a definition_scope vs runtime_scope nuance (see source_scope / runtime_scope candidates).

description: Actionability of the unit for cross-project sync. Separate from scope: a 'universal' unit may still need sanitization before it can be moved.
rationale: User Q4 explicitly flagged portability as separate from scope. Confirmed clean coverage by every Wave 1 agent with no edge cases requiring a 4th value. 'sanitize-then-portable' is the most common state — it carries the most signal for sync tooling (tells the sync engine: copy + edit, not copy-as-is).

description: Human-readable canonical identifier for the unit, matching filename slug where applicable.
rationale: Universal identifier field. D4a noted that frontmatter name field matches filename slug in all cases in JASON-OS — makes automated parsing reliable. D4b-3 flagged frontmatter schema drift where some older files omit name.

description: Relative path to the unit's primary file or directory from the repo root.
rationale: Fundamental lookup key for sync tooling. Already present in all JSONL records from Wave 1 agents.

description: Primary category of the unit within the JASON-OS taxonomy.
rationale: Load-bearing for per-category field applicability. Every Wave 1 agent defined the type of unit they scanned. D2 flagged team as structurally distinct from agent (HTML comment metadata vs YAML frontmatter).

description: One-sentence human-readable description of what the unit does.
rationale: Already present in all Wave 1 JSONL records. Essential for human-readable synthesis and search.

description: List of other registry units this unit directly calls or depends on at runtime.
rationale: Core of the dependency graph Piece 2 needs to design. D3 noted hard vs soft dependency distinction is missing from current schema — see dependency_hardness field.

description: Lifecycle state of the unit.
rationale: Surfaced by D4a, D4b-1, D4b-2, D7, D8. Already present in frontmatter on many memory files. D1a introduced stub_level for skills — normalized into status here with 'stub' as a value. D7 used complete/active/archived for research sessions. D8 used active/complete/deferred/gated for planning artifacts. Merging into a single field is cleaner than 4 separate enums.

description: Free-text explanation of WHY a unit requires sanitization or is not portable. Provides human-readable context for the portability enum value.
rationale: D4a explicitly recommended this field: 'would explain WHY a memory needs sanitization (e.g., contains project-specific stack note on line 6)'. Currently embedded in the notes field which is unstructured. A dedicated field enables automated extraction.

description: Reference to a prior unit that this unit replaces. Enables version lineage tracking across renames and consolidations.
rationale: D4a identified the feedback_verify_not_grep vs feedback_grep_vs_understanding naming divergence as the highest-priority schema gap. Without this field, supersession relationships are invisible to automated sync. D4b-1 confirmed the same pattern (ack/interactive-gates/bulk-accept redundancy cluster needs supersedes to trace consolidation).

description: Reference to the unit that replaces this unit. The inverse of supersedes.
rationale: Pair with supersedes. Both directions needed for graph traversal: supersedes tells you what this replaced, superseded_by tells you what replaced this. A deprecated memory file with superseded_by set can be auto-excluded from sync candidates.

description: Array of related unit names that are conceptually linked but not dependencies or supersession relationships.
rationale: D4a: 'feedback_explain_before_decide already cross-references 3 other memories inline. Making this structured would enable graph traversal.' D4b-1 confirmed as related_memories[]. D4b-3 noted project_sync_mechanism_principles has a Memory links section naming 5 files. Surfaced as 'related_memories' in D4a/D4b-1 — normalized to 'related' here as it applies to all unit types.

description: Origin tracking: where the unit was ported from and at what version. Enables bidirectional sync without losing provenance.
rationale: D1a: 'pr-review documents this explicitly in frontmatter (Lineage field). Other skills don't have it yet, but it would be valuable for every ported skill.' D1b recommended version_lineage: 'upstream vs jason-os-port vs original'. D4b-3 observed port_lineage documented in pr-review. For sync, knowing 'this JASON-OS skill was ported from SoNash v2.3' enables diff-based update detection.

description: The portability scope of the unit's source code / definition — independent of how it behaves at runtime.
rationale: D6 identified cache.go as the canonical example: the source code is universal/portable, but its runtime behavior writes to a machine-specific path (~/.claude/statusline/cache-jason-os/). D2 noted the same tension for ephemeral runtime vs universal definition. A single scope field conflates these. Most critical for tools and hooks.

description: The portability scope of the unit's runtime behavior — what paths it touches, what state it writes, what credentials it needs at execution time.
rationale: Companion to source_scope. D2 flagged that agent team definitions are universal at definition time but ephemeral at runtime. D6 flagged cache.go writes to machine-scoped paths despite universal source. Together source_scope + runtime_scope replace the ambiguity of a single scope field for tools and hooks.

description: Array of sub-units that compose this composite unit, each with a role label. For skills with REFERENCE.md + companion files, hooks with lib dependencies, tools with multi-file source.
rationale: D1a surfaced companion_files as a candidate; D3 identified hook-to-lib-helper relationships; D6 catalogued multi-file Go tool where all files compose the tool. This generalizes all those patterns into component_units[] with role labels (e.g., {name: 'REFERENCE.md', role: 'companion-doc'} or {name: 'sanitize-error.cjs', role: 'lib-dep'}). D11's upcoming hub-analysis output will need this field.

description: Claude Code hook event type this unit listens to (hooks only).
rationale: D3: 'should be a first-class field for indexing' rather than embedded in existing_metadata.event. Already extracted into existing_metadata but needs promotion. Load-bearing for hook sync: a PreToolUse hook that blocks operations needs different handling than a SessionStart informational hook.

description: Tool name regex filter for the hook event. Null means fires on all invocations of that event type.
rationale: D3 identified this as load-bearing for porting decisions. A hook with matcher=null has very different blast radius than one with matcher='^[Bb]ash$'. Already in existing_metadata but should be a first-class field.

description: Additional filter condition beyond matcher for the hook event, expressed as a tool call pattern string.
rationale: D3: used sparingly in JASON-OS (once out of 7 entries) but 'more important at SoNash scale' with 25+ hooks. The if_condition in settings.json (e.g., 'Bash(git push *)') provides more granular filtering. Needed to reconstruct settings.json hook entries precisely during sync.

description: Boolean: whether the hook allows Claude to continue when it exits with a non-zero code (fail-open) or blocks (fail-closed).
rationale: D3: 'Load-bearing for porting decisions — blocking vs fail-open is a critical distinction.' A hook that exits 2 to block an operation (block-push-to-main) has completely different risk profile than a monitoring hook with continueOnError:true. Already in existing_metadata but must be a first-class field.

description: Semantics of the hook's exit codes: what happens on 0, 2, non-zero.
rationale: D3: 'Maps exit code semantics (0=allow, 2=block); not captured today.' Distinct from continue_on_error — this describes what the hook intends, not just what Claude Code does when it exits non-zero. A 'warn' hook might always exit 0 but print to stderr.

description: Boolean: whether this hook spawns a subprocess asynchronously (fire-and-forget) rather than waiting for it to complete.
rationale: D3: 'commit-tracker spawns mid-session-alerts.js asynchronously — this is an unusual pattern worth flagging.' Async spawns don't block the Claude Code turn but introduce race conditions. Important for sync safety analysis: an async hook is harder to test than a synchronous one.

description: Environment variable name that disables this hook when set (e.g., SKIP_GATES=1). Null if no kill switch exists.
rationale: D3: 'SKIP_GATES=1 pattern appears in large-file-gate and settings-guardian; a standard field would enable audit of which hooks are bypassable.' Security-relevant: if a hook is bypassable, the bypass mechanism should be documented and auditable. Different from continueOnError — this is a deliberate operator escape hatch.

description: Array of file paths (or path patterns) that this unit reads from or writes to in .claude/state/ or similar persistent state locations.
rationale: D1a: 'has_state_file: boolean + state file path pattern. 5 of 7 skills write named state files to .claude/state/' D1b recommended state_files list. D3: 'state_files_written: string[] — essential for dependency mapping across hooks.' State files must stay project-local, not travel with units during sync. Knowing which units write which state files enables cross-unit dependency analysis.

description: List of named agent types (subagent_type values) that this unit dispatches. Critical for 'does this skill work in this environment?' assessment.
rationale: D1a: 'An environment without a contrarian-challenger agent definition can't run deep-research Phase 3.' deep-research spawns 8+ named agent types; brainstorm spawns 3; checkpoint spawns 0. This field enables automated compatibility checking: before syncing a skill, verify all its spawned agents exist in the target environment.

description: Array of external services, APIs, CLIs, or MCP servers this unit depends on.
rationale: Already in Wave 1 JSONL schema. D2 recommended splitting into mcp_tools vs external_services. D9 recommended required_secrets as a separate field. Keeping external_refs as the general array but adding sub-type or splitting as noted.

description: Array of MCP server tool names this unit requires. Separate from external_refs: MCP dependencies fail silently if the server isn't configured, making them a distinct portability concern.
rationale: D2: 'MCP tool refs in tools field — worth flagging as external_refs category distinct from agent-to-agent dependencies.' D3 flagged mcp_dependencies as distinct from tools in agent YAML. An environment without the memory MCP server configured cannot run checkpoint --mcp; without context7 MCP, deep-research-searcher loses a tool. Currently merged into external_refs.

description: Array of secret names (e.g., SONAR_TOKEN) that must be provisioned in the target environment for this unit to function. Distinct from dependencies — secrets require operator action in GitHub Settings, not just file edits.
rationale: D9: 'These require GitHub Settings operator action, not just file edits.' GITHUB_TOKEN (built-in) vs SONAR_TOKEN (must provision) is a critical distinction. CI workflows especially benefit from this field: a sync that copies a workflow without provisioning SONAR_TOKEN will silently fail at runtime.

description: Array of external CLI tools or binaries required at runtime (e.g., gh, gitleaks, go, node, bash). Distinct from mcp_dependencies and required_secrets.
rationale: D3: gitleaks binary dependency in pre-commit script. D6: build.sh requires go and bash. D5: scripts that shell out to git. A unit that requires gitleaks binary cannot sync to an environment without it. Currently captured in external_refs but mixed with API/MCP dependencies.

description: Boolean: whether this unit's source must be compiled/built before it is operational.
rationale: D6: 'Go source requires go build; scripts do not. Critical for sync decisions (can you sync source and run it, or must you build first?)' The compiled jason-statusline-v2.exe is gitignored but operationally required. A sync that copies only source without triggering a build leaves the tool non-functional.

description: Boolean: whether a pre-built binary artifact exists on disk alongside the source (typically gitignored).
rationale: D6: 'The compiled binary (jason-statusline-v2.exe) is gitignored but operationally required. Knowing whether a pre-built binary exists on disk matters for is-this-tool-usable-right-now assessment.' This is a runtime state observation, not a schema intrinsic — it would need to be computed at scan time.

description: Path (possibly ~-relative) where the tool installs or copies itself to at build/install time. Different from path (source location).
rationale: D6: 'Sync must understand install targets to know what to rebuild after pulling.' jason-statusline installs to ~/.claude/statusline/. If a sync pulls updated source but doesn't re-run the install, the running binary is stale.

description: Boolean: whether this unit requires a gitignored secret config file to be operational (e.g., config.local.toml with API key).
rationale: D6: 'Flags tools that require a gitignored secret file before they are operational. Would prevent false-positive ready-to-use assessments during sync.' The statusline tool requires a weather API key in config.local.toml which is gitignored and machine-specific.

description: Identifier of the Claude Code session in which this unit was created or last significantly modified. May be a UUID or a human-readable session label.
rationale: D4a recommended: 'would let us trace when each memory was created.' D4b-1: 'present in 5 files; enables tracing a memory to its originating session for audit/context.' D4b-2 flagged inconsistent values: UUIDs in some files, human-readable Session #NNN labels in others. Normalization needed before this field is useful.

description: Staleness indicator from the Claude Code memory system (e.g., system-reminder annotations like 'This memory is 2 days old').
rationale: D4b-2: 'captures the system-reminder staleness flag present on 2 files.' This is a Claude Code platform behavior, not user-authored metadata. Worth capturing during scan to flag memories that may need refreshing, but it's an observed property, not a schema field the user sets.

description: Boolean: whether this unit is an append-only log that should never be overwritten during sync (only appended to).
rationale: D4a: 'true for session-end-learnings.md. Would guide sync behavior (never overwrite, only append).' An append-only log in the source repo cannot simply be copied over an existing append-only log in the target — it needs merge logic. This field makes that handling explicit.

description: Sub-type of memory file within the memory taxonomy.
rationale: D4a established the 4-type taxonomy (user/feedback/project/reference). D4b-3 identified the tenet convention (t3_ prefix, type:reference in frontmatter but semantically distinct). D4b-1 noted index files need their own sub-type. The memory_type field disambiguates within the broader type:memory category.

description: Filename prefix category for memory files (feedback_, project_, reference_, t[N]_, user_). The prefix is the primary convention signal, not the type field.
rationale: D4b-3: 'The type field alone is insufficient — t3_ has type:reference but is semantically distinct from reference_pr_review_integrations.md.' The prefix encodes a convention that the type field does not capture. Tenet files (t[N]_) have an implied taxonomy (t1_, t2_, siblings) that prefix_convention makes explicit.

description: For tenet files (t[N]_ prefix), the numeric tenet identifier.
rationale: D4b-3: 't3 prefix appears to indicate Tenet #3.' Only relevant for the t[N]_ memory convention. Could be derived from prefix_convention, but an explicit integer field enables sorting/sequencing tenet files in order.

description: Boolean: whether a canonical counterpart to this user-home memory exists in .claude/canonical-memory/.
rationale: All three D4b agents used this as a primary analysis field. D4b-1 found 76% gap rate (16/21 user-home memories not in canonical). D4b-2 found 90% gap rate (19/21). This field is the primary input to the sync gap analysis — it directly answers 'what needs to be promoted to canonical?'

description: Free-text or structured description of the portable principles extractable from an otherwise non-portable unit (e.g., project-type memories with embedded general methodology).
rationale: D4b-3: 'Two project memories contain portable elements — the classification framework in project_cross_locale_config is a portable methodology even though the specific file list is project-specific.' Currently invisible to automated sync: a not-portable project memory may contain a principle worth canonicalizing. Piece 2 schema design could use this to flag manual review items.

description: List of named phases, steps, or infrastructure items explicitly marked DEFERRED within this unit.
rationale: D1b: 'session-begin and session-end have significant deferred surfaces; knowing this upfront signals sync risk.' A skill with many deferred sections may depend on infrastructure that doesn't exist yet in the target environment. More specific than the generic notes field.

description: List of specific systems or hooks the unit waits on that have not yet been implemented. Distinct from dependencies: these are future-gated, not current requirements.
rationale: D1b: 'Distinct from dependencies because these are future-gated, not current requirements.' Example: skill-audit's self-audit.js references a Loop 2 hook that doesn't exist yet. Knowing which units have deferred infrastructure prevents false-positive 'ready to sync' assessments.

description: Whether this unit is a production implementation, a v0 stub with documented upgrade trigger, or a planned placeholder.
rationale: D1a: 'add-debt is explicitly a v0 stub with a documented upgrade trigger. This is a distinct portability concern — stubs may need full replacement in SoNash rather than copy-forward.' Merged into status field as the 'stub' value, but surfacing it here as a separate candidate in case Piece 2 wants finer granularity.

description: How companion documentation files are organized relative to the main unit file.
rationale: D1a: 'flat (REFERENCE.md at skill root) vs subdirectory (pr-review's reference/ dir). Affects tooling that auto-discovers companion docs.' Subdirectory layout requires a different glob pattern than flat layout. Currently captured in notes.

description: Structured list of files or directories this unit writes as outputs (path pattern + description + retained vs gitignored).
rationale: D1a: 'deep-research output structure is complex (4 retained files + gitignored findings/ and challenges/ dirs). This is important for sync: gitignored intermediates must stay gitignored in the target repo.' D2 recommended output_paths as a first-class field. Gitignored artifacts should not travel during sync.

description: What paths/files this unit reads as inputs at runtime (not static dependencies but runtime data reads).
rationale: D2 recommended input_paths as a first-class field for data-flow dependency graph. D8 noted research_inputs (list of research dir slugs consumed) as a planning-specific variant. Currently inferred from reading full file bodies — expensive at scale.

description: The temporal persistence of this unit's runtime instances: ephemeral (per-session) vs persistent (daemon) vs per-invocation.
rationale: D2: 'The schema should distinguish between a unit's definition scope (universal) and its runtime lifecycle (ephemeral). Current R-frpg enum conflates these.' The research-plan-team is universal in definition but ephemeral in runtime — each spawn creates and destroys an instance. Hooks are per-invocation. The statusline tool is persistent (runs as a daemon).

description: For agents that are part of a sequential pipeline, the phase number or label (e.g., Phase 2.5, Phase 3.97).
rationale: D2: 'With 40 agents potentially spanning many phases, the phase number in the description is the primary sort key for pipeline sequencing. Extracting it into a dedicated field makes downstream analysis faster.' Enables automatic pipeline sequencing without reading agent descriptions.

description: LLM model identifier used by this agent (e.g., claude-sonnet-4-6, claude-opus-4).
rationale: D2: 'sonnet vs opus directly affects token cost.' Only one JASON-OS agent uses opus (research-plan-team planner member). At SoNash scale there may be more opus agents; these are disproportionately expensive and worth flagging in the inventory for sync cost analysis.

description: Maximum turns allowed for this agent per invocation. Absence means unbounded (cost risk).
rationale: D2: 'maxTurns is absent on 2 of 8 agents. Absence means unbounded.' A missing maxTurns is a cost and safety control gap. Important for sync: if a SoNash agent has maxTurns:50 and JASON-OS doesn't, the JASON-OS version is riskier.

description: List of tools this agent is explicitly prevented from using. Absence vs empty list is a meaningful distinction.
rationale: D2: 'The schema should use null vs [] vs [Agent] to distinguish: not declared / explicitly none / explicitly blocked.' Two JASON-OS agents lack disallowedTools — a security posture gap. DisallowedTools:['Agent'] prevents nested spawning; its absence allows it.

description: Visual color identifier for the agent as displayed in Claude Code UI.
rationale: D2: 'Visual identity in Claude Code UI; worth preserving.' Appears on only 2 of 8 JASON-OS agents. Cosmetic but worth capturing to preserve UI identity on sync.

description: JavaScript module system used by this script or library.
rationale: D5: 'Critical given the mixed environment. The CJS/ESM distinction is architecturally significant and can't be inferred from extension alone.' A .js file may be CJS or ESM depending on nearest package.json. This distinction matters for sync: ESM scripts cannot use require(); CJS cannot use top-level await.

description: Boolean: whether this script is invoked directly (entry point) vs only imported by other scripts (library module).
rationale: D5: 'distinguishes scripts invoked directly from library-only modules.' Entry-point scripts need executable permissions, CLI argument parsing, and error handling suitable for direct invocation. Library modules need only export clean APIs.

description: Boolean: whether this unit spawns external processes via exec, execFile, spawn, or similar.
rationale: D5: 'flags files that spawn subprocesses (relevant for security audit).' D3 noted execFile in commit-tracker is easy to miss. A script that shells out to git or gitleaks has a different security profile than one that doesn't.

description: Primary programming language of this unit's source files.
rationale: D6: 'already in existing_metadata, but worth promoting to top-level for tools.' Go vs bash vs toml have fundamentally different handling requirements for sync, build, and execution.

description: CI workflow trigger events (push, pull_request, schedule, workflow_dispatch, etc.).
rationale: D9: 'enables filtering by when a workflow runs.' PR-gate workflows (pull_request) must be ported; scheduled cron workflows are optional for a fork. This field enables automated filtering of essential vs optional CI.

description: Explicit list of field names or config keys that must be replaced with project-specific values when porting this unit.
rationale: D9: 'explicit list of fields needing replacement on port' — sonar.projectKey, sonar.organization, name. Currently these are identified by reading file bodies. A pre-computed list enables automated find-replace at sync time without requiring a human to re-read the file.

description: Boolean: whether this config or workflow references or consumes secrets (beyond GITHUB_TOKEN).
rationale: D9: 'flags configs that reference or consume secrets.' sonar-project.properties and settings.json both consume secrets. A secret-bearing config requires operator provisioning steps during sync that a secret-free config does not.

description: Structured array of pinned GitHub Actions references used by this CI workflow, with action name, SHA, and human-readable tag.
rationale: D9: 'structured extraction of pinned actions.' All 7 JASON-OS workflows use SHA-pinned actions. At SoNash sync time, verifying that action SHAs are current (or matching) is a security check. Currently buried in workflow YAML bodies.

description: For research sessions: whether this is a brainstorm-only, formal deep-research, deep-plan, or hybrid session.
rationale: D7: 'jason-os-mvp is hybrid research+planning.' Hybrid sessions produce both RESEARCH_OUTPUT.md and PLAN-type artifacts. A brainstorm-only session has minimal files; a formal deep-research session has 4+ structured artifacts. Session type determines what fields to expect and how to parse the session.

description: For research sessions: research depth level (L0-brainstorm through L4-exhaustive).
rationale: D7: 'differentiates brainstorm from formal research.' L1 exhaustive sessions deploy 8+ agents and produce 50+ claims; L0 brainstorm sessions produce one BRAINSTORM.md. Depth determines scan strategy and field expectations.

description: Sub-type of planning artifact: what kind of plan this represents.
rationale: D8: 'plan_scope: milestone | research-program | deferral-registry | session-bookmark | port-ledger | execution-handoff.' A RESUME.md (session-bookmark) has completely different sync semantics than a PLAN.md (milestone). This field enables plan-type-specific sync rules without reading the file body.

description: For each dependency: whether it is hard (required, will fail without it) or soft (graceful degradation if absent).
rationale: D3: 'several hooks use try { require(...) } catch { fallback } — soft dependencies with graceful degradation. The schema dependencies array doesn't distinguish hard vs soft.' A soft dependency on gitleaks means the hook degrades gracefully if gitleaks isn't installed; a hard dependency means it fails. Critical for portability assessment.

description: Boolean: whether this skill includes a self-audit script or phase (signals a quality tier distinction).
rationale: D1b: 'skill-audit and skill-creator reference scripts/skills/*/self-audit.js; other skills don't. This signals a quality tier distinction.' Skills with self-audit phases are more mature and have better automated quality gates.

description: List of Node.js or Python scripts actually invoked by this skill (not just referenced as neighbors). More useful than dependencies for sync risk assessment.
rationale: D1b: 'more useful than dependencies for sync risk assessment.' todo invokes 2 scripts; session-end invokes 1; skill-audit invokes 1 (self-audit.js). These are execution dependencies, not conceptual neighbors. If the script doesn't exist in the target environment, the skill breaks.

description: Boolean: whether this unit is depended upon by many other units (a hub in the dependency graph). Computed from depended_on_count.
rationale: D11 (hub-analysis, expected output): convergence-loop is the most-referenced-by-others skill (4+ callers). D1a noted convergence-loop as 'the infrastructure primitive.' Hub units have higher sync priority — breaking a hub breaks many dependents. Computed field, not user-set.

description: Integer: how many other units depend on this unit. The raw count from which is_hub is derived.
rationale: Companion to is_hub. The raw count enables threshold-based is_hub calculation. D1a noted density pattern: convergence-loop has 4+ callers, add-debt and checkpoint have 0-1. The count determines sync priority ordering.

description: For units that are copies of another canonical unit (e.g., safe-fs.js copied into skill subdirs), the path to the canonical source.
rationale: D5: 'safe-fs.js documents that it is copied verbatim into skill subdirs. A canonical_location field would prevent treating skill copies as distinct entries.' Without this field, sync tools may treat the copy as an independent unit, leading to sync of an outdated copy when the canonical version was updated.

description: Boolean or path: whether corresponding test files exist for this unit.
rationale: D5: 'flag if there are corresponding test files (todos-mutations.js was extracted specifically for testability).' A unit with test coverage is safer to sync than one without. The presence of tests also signals a maturity tier.

description: For CI workflows: the OS runner used (e.g., ubuntu-latest).
rationale: D9: 'all 7 JASON-OS workflows use ubuntu-latest; useful for cross-OS portability analysis.' If a workflow requires ubuntu-latest and the target repo's CI uses windows-latest, a port flag is needed.

description: Free-text field for observations, caveats, or nuances not captured by structured fields.
rationale: Already present in all Wave 1 JSONL records. The catch-all for observations that don't fit structured fields. Important to preserve even as more structured fields are added — some nuances will always require prose.
